dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for chest pain with RFD at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
It's chest pain on the left side. It's been there since this morning around 7 o'clock.
It feels sharp and has gone into my left shoulder, but I used to have pain there before too.
Can you tell me your name and where we are right now?
My name is Tom, and we're in Richmond, apartment 206.
Do you know what day it is and when you had your last meal or drink?
The day is March 13th. I haven't had anything to eat or drink today yet.
What led up to the chest pain here today, and do you recognize the nurse you were speaking to earlier?
Yep, the nurse was helping me. I've had this left side pain since 7 a.m., and it's getting a bit better now after they gave me something.
All right, let me assess your airway now.
Go ahead.
Your airway is clear and patent. We're now going to check your breathing.
Let's evaluate your rate and depth of breathing, Mr. Tom.
Your rate is 16 per minute with normal depth. No signs of respiratory distress or labored breathing.
Mr. Tom, we'll now check your breath sounds and chest movement.
No abnormal breath sounds, no cyanosis. Breath sounds are clear and chest moves evenly.
We'll now examine your pulse and skin appearance.
Your pulse is 102, strong and regular. No abnormalities in rhythm.
Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color. No cyanosis or pallor noted.
We're about to take your blood pressure. Is that okay with you, Mr. Tom?
Your blood pressure is 175 over 85. Slightly elevated, but nothing too concerning at this time.
Let's check your blood glucose level now.
Your blood sugar is 280. It's a little high, but given your insulin-dependent diabetes, that's not unusual.
Skin remains warm and dry with no changes in temperature.
No rashes, bruises, or infections noted. Skin turgor is normal.
You noted that the left-shoulder pain isn't new. Has it changed today compared to usual?
A little, but it's less intense now. I had some meds from the nurse earlier, which helped.
Got it. On a scale from 1 to 10, how would you rate the chest pain now?
About 5. Sharp, stabbing, and not constant, but still noticeable.
I see you have a past history of high blood pressure, diabetes, and a stroke. How have you been doing with those today?
Good so far. I'm on Humalog and Metoprolol. No issues with those.
Your stroke left you with some left-side paralysis and chronic pain. Has your left side functioned differently today?
No, it's the same. I can't move it, and the left-side pain isn't new. Just the chest is new.
Our initial heart assessment showed a normal rhythm, but we're now doing a 4-lead ECG.
Your 12-lead ECG has been completed. Findings show a sinus tachycardia with a possible fascicular block.
We've completed the 4-lead ECG for ongoing monitoring.
A full 12-lead ECG was done. No signs of an acute heart attack.
Let's start an IV in your left wrist-will that work for you?
The IV is in and running. It's good to go if you need meds or fluids.
Based on your alertness and stable vitals, we're using protocol 2-1 for a suspected cardiac cause.
Per dispatch notes, 324mg of aspirin was already provided-no need for more right now.
Let me confirm your breathing once more.
Your breathing rate is 16 with normal depth. Still no distress.
Your pulse is now 103-steady and regular.
Blood pressure now reads 169 over 82. No significant changes.
Oxygen saturation is at 95. No signs of hypoxia.
Another glucose check shows a reading of zero-expected since you haven't eaten today.
We'll be using a stretcher with a 5-point harness for your movement to the ambulance.
You're now safely strapped in with a secure harness for transport.
ECG findings are stable, showing again that there's no acute cardiac change.
You mentioned a prior appointment at Chippenham. Would you like to head there?
Yes, that's where my appointment is. I want to go to Chippenham.
Full patient history and care plan are in transit. A staff RN is ready to receive you.
All care and documentation have been handed off. You'll be admitted as a delayed transport.
You're being carefully transported to Chippenham via stretcher and harness.
You're now en route to the hospital for further evaluation.
